item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images used in this article were created for the article and thus are cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What's the (in-universe) point of censoring an envelope while leaving clear one of the most distinctive street addresses in history?

IRL, I understand you need to tell the readers that this was a child's idea of an all-powerful blackmail target. But a Foundation who wanted to keep that target secret wouldn't leave such a blatant clue.
You could achieve nearly the same effect, and less implied incompetence, by leaving only the final line (presumed "Washington, DC 20037").

Afterthought Edit: I just realized that this ransom letter could apply equally well to any of the last three administrations. No idea why that amuses me as much as it does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if they could use 085 for testing with this, could she go into the letters and tell them if there's something there that they cant see or something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if they could use 085 for testing with this, could she go into the letters and tell them if there's something there that they cant see or something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
we're apparently dealing with something that is simultaneously stupid and omniscient

This describes a lot of my favorite SCiPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Congrats for writing an SCP that does not give a cheap scare while simultaniously killing me wit stress.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"But as illogical as this will sound, we're apparently dealing with something that is simultaneously stupid and omniscient"

I was feeling rather meh about this article until the above phrase sealed the deal. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At which point 682 suddenly appears in Tokyo, fairly delirious and surrounded by vast quantities of ink, shredded newspaper, and rubber cement

1020 remains dormant for 6 months. Whenever 682 is mentioned around it, it immediately starts writing a letter addressed to the one who mentioned him

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not trying to kill the Foundation, it's trying to get away from them. As was mentioned earlier, they've been thwarting its sole purpose, and it wants to get back to requesting strange things or else [family member here] gets it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After that escape attempt, I'd consider listed it as Keter. It wants to kill the Foundation, and it's actively trying.

There's no accounting for teleportation.

Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I saw a couple suggestions about the agent retrieving the letter being the recipient.

I can't help but think this would be easily thwarted by having more than one agent pick it up. The anomalous effects only happen when the recipient reads the letter. The agent can just look at the envelope and go, "Oh, hey, this is addressed to me. Bob, you open it."

Unless it can be addressed to more than one person, but that could still be thwarted via taking it back to Sector █ and having someone there read it.

Possibly it could address the letter to "SCP Foundation Agent" or something generic like that, but I don't think it's smart enough to do that.

That said, I liked it.

item.title: Hm
item.content:encoded: Out of curiosity, how long did it take you to make that ransom letter?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one. Smart and stupid, powerful but limited. Honest (returns the people if conditions are met) yet dishonest (steals supplies).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, the second definition says "very great or seemingly unlimited."
But yeah, I definitely used the word incorrectly by most standards just because I liked the way it sounded. I just happened to later find one dictionary definition that agreed with me, sort of. Down with dictionaries. Up with amusing phrases.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You will notice that the definition does not have restrictions. if it did, the phrase "omniscient about the back of my hand" instead of "I know that like the back of my hand." Extensive knowledge isn't all knowing.

I just feel that when you have scp-343 to measure things against, your standards are a little higher.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, darn. What's the word for "knowing nearly everything about everything within an unknown geographical range at a certain given moment?"
World English Dictionary seems to think that word is "omniscient."

omniscient(ɒmˈnɪsɪənt)

— adj
1. having infinite knowledge or understanding
2. having very great or seemingly unlimited knowledge

[C17: from Medieval Latin omnisciens, from Latin omni- + scīre to know]

om'niscience

— n

om'nisciently

— adv

BOOM. Diction-pwned. (I boast in jest. Truthfully, even I had a small eye twitch while writing this. It's like saying "very unique." But scientist =/ English major, and he's - er, I mean I'm writing for effect.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, there are degrees of infinity (seriously. some infinities are larger than others. math is weird, yo.), but your base point is taken and agreed with in the context of this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
….omniscient - for an unknown value of omniscience…

Arggh! There is no such thing as part of infinite. It's still infinite!

No vote for a scientist badly using the word.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well done goosey. This is excellent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I got this. That is to say, I've created a backup copy. Just in case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-067 for starters. I feel like some others might work, but since 1020 isn't controllable, testing becomes extremely hard. The best cross-testing would have to happen by 1020's choice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually brought that up in the forum. Wikidot doesn't let us.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Solid writing, but the idea itself is a bit too mundane for me. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you liked it. And no, because that'd probably defeat the purpose of a ransom note.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I will say I think I've officially made one you cannot possibly use against 682.

Brainwash some poor shmuck into thinking that 682 is a beloved family member. Set up the postal system so that for mail dropped off at the nearest mailbox, mail to said shmuck gets sent directly to him, but all other mail gets forwarded to the foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I too enjoyed the "all knowing, all stupid" angle, and the change-up you (apparently) did to make it steal and teleport in supplies rather than having a self-regenerating supply.

I have to ask, is there ever any sort of return address or other identifiable marking on the outside?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We need to be able to +1 comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
we're apparently dealing with something that is simultaneously stupid and omniscient

Heh heh heh. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Out of gratitude, the Foundation gives 1020 its own office.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this one. Clever idea, well executed, and the letter at the end really pulled it together.

Random idea: the intended recipient of the letter is the agent responsible for retrieving it from the mailbox. Could be interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: K1lL tHe 05 cOUnc1l Or yOU w1Ll nEvEr SEE yOUr 682 AgA1n

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The article says the abductees come back with no memory of the time they were missing.
As for geographical boundaries, here's my simple explanation that I just came up with: it only speaks English. And doesn't know how to do international mail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I'll at least kill the "repairs newspapers" bit, and have it steal individual stamps and place them in its own book rather than just regenerate them. I don't like infinite supplies either, though teleportation theft doesn't bug me for some reason.

I thought about the Foundation having to stock it, but for storytelling reasons, I kind of want to leave it so it looks like the item has no thought except "keep writing letters to hurt random people." I don't want to indicate self-awareness in the letters until that escape attempt at the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That might be good, but if the threats are idle, then the Foundation just says no, and this item is now boring. So it'd have to be able to do that somehow.

Perhaps it mind controls the nearest few people to the kidnapping target into being kidnappers? With no real consideration to how well this would work? Eh, that might be a bit too powerful. Still, I agree that the bits that just function because they do need some improvement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seems interesting, but I have a few questions.

Have the people who were abducted been questioned as to where they were when the letter took them? That was one of the first things I thought of.

Can it only demand ransoms from people within the United States? That sounds kind of arbitrary to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have a lot of issue with the whole unending supply, and magically puts itself together part. What if it had to be fed supplies, or it would start generating ransom demands for them ? "giV me mor pper, or yore dog gets it"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aw, shoot. I'm 0 for 2 on the Bright side. Just out of curiosity, was it something in the execution, or the concept itself that didn't work for you?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Any ideas for improvement on the abduction/return part in particular?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe there are occasional misspellings?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Might try modifying the ransom note, but not much else really springs to mind for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm also curious which ones you think would be good for cross-testing. Obviously this thing can't be aimed, so I can't think of any particular cross-tests that would work, but I'm open for suggestions.

I will say I think I've officially made one you cannot possibly use against 682. But then again, maybe that's why the Foundation reads all the letters: in case one is addressed to someone with a name written in a hostile-looking alien language residing at Site [REDACTED]. Who that letter would threaten is anyone's guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Which ones in particular were you interested in cross-testing it with? Might end up being worthwhile.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP really makes me think of cross testing with other SCPs, which is a great way to do it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I personally think it doesn't have a game. Sending ransom notes is what it does because it's what it does. A ransom note needs demands, so it makes demands. The demand has to be for something the extortee would otherwise be very adverse to doing, so that's what the demands are like. But it doesn't need anything, since it's self-repairing, self-re-supplying, and indestructible. So it just has to make its demands something that can't be reversed/undone. It tried to blackmail the President because it's tired of the Foundation thwarting its purpose in existence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd have loved to have seen a letter that told an MTF reading it to free the SCP or let the next letter reach it's target.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a decent point. I like the clipped newspaper stuff, but it might be good to spread the general amateurishness beyond its escape attempt. Perhaps have cruder methods for its function? Not sure how that would work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the escape attempt, the implication that the object is omniscient and stupid, and the apparent randomness of the demands; but the "magical" way the letter is written, the person is abducted and returned took me out of it. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see what yer going for, it just doesn't work for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought this was good, but the letter at the end was the kicker.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice. The escape attempt was a good touch. I must say, I'm wondering what game this thing is playing at. It may not be playing at it very well, but what if its demands are building towards something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty interesting. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Weird and original. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. This is interesting.
